literarySources.title: The Divine Comedy
literarySources.author: Dante Alighieri
literarySources.passage: All hope abandon, ye who enter in.
literarySources.passageContext: Inscription above the gates of Hell, Divine Comedy — Inferno

literarySources.title: Faust
literarySources.author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
literarySources.passage: Whoever constantly and restlessly yearns — him we may redeem.
literarySources.passageContext: Mephistopheles, on his wager with Faust

literarySources.title: Moby-Dick
literarySources.author: Herman Melville
literarySources.passage: It is a fair wind, the West Wind, the loveliest wind of all.
literarySources.passageContext: Ishmael, reflecting on the sea after returning from his voyage

literarySources.title: Demian
literarySources.author: Hermann Hesse
literarySources.passage: The bird fights its way out of the egg. The egg is the world.
literarySources.passageContext: Emil Sinclair, recalling a story told by Demian

literarySources.title: Don Quixote
literarySources.author: Miguel de Cervantes
literarySources.passage: When life itself seems lunatic, who knows where madness lies?
literarySources.passageContext: Don Quixote, on the nature of his quest

literarySources.title: The Stranger (L'Étranger)
literarySources.author: Albert Camus
literarySources.passage: Mother died today. Or, maybe, yesterday; I can't be sure.
literarySources.passageContext: Meursault's opening lines — establishing his emotional detachment

literarySources.title: Wuthering Heights
literarySources.author: Emily Brontë
literarySources.passage: Whatever our souls are made of, his and mine are the same.
literarySources.passageContext: Catherine Earnshaw to Nelly Dean, on her bond with Heathcliff

literarySources.title: The Metamorphosis
literarySources.author: Franz Kafka
literarySources.passage: One morning, when Gregor Samsa woke from uneasy dreams, he found himself transformed in his bed into a monstrous vermin.
literarySources.passageContext: Opening line of The Metamorphosis — Gregor's literal transformation

literarySources.title: Crime and Punishment
literarySources.author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
literarySources.passage: Pain and suffering are always inevitable for a large intelligence and a deep heart.
literarySources.passageContext: Raskolnikov's internal justification for his crime

literarySources.title: Dream of the Red Chamber (红楼梦)
literarySources.author: Cao Xueqin
literarySources.passage: Truth from fiction — Dream of the Red Chamber; happiness from sorrow — Flowers beneath the Mirror.
literarySources.passageContext: A couplet from the novel's opening verses, referring to its nature as both a dream and a mirror of reality

literarySources.title: The Flight of Earth (iqug-ui Han-Yang)
literarySources.author: Yi Sang (이grammar상)
literarySources.passage: If there were no earth, I would not have to love.
literarySources.passageContext: From 'Fog' (안개), expressing the speaker's entangled relationship with existence and dissolution

literarySources.title: The Odyssey
literarySources.author: Homer
literarySources.passage: Tell me, O muse, of that ingenious hero who travelled far and wide.
literarySources.passageContext: Opening invocation — Odysseus's long journey home after the Trojan War

literarySources.title: In a Grove
literarySources.author: Ryunosuke Akutagawa
literarySources.passageContext: The woodcutter's testimony — opening one of seven contradictory accounts of a single murder

literarySources.title: The Wings
literarySources.author: Yi Sang
literarySources.passage: Have you ever seen a stuffed genius?
literarySources.passageContext: Opening line — the painter Yong-chol confronting the decay of his own talent and identity

literarySources.title: Hell Screen
literarySources.author: Ryunosuke Akutagawa
literarySources.passage: I am certain that he did it to punish the twisted personality of an artist who would go so far as to burn a carriage and kill a human being to complete the painting of a screen.
literarySources.passageContext: The narrator revealing the Lord of Horikawa's motive for burning Yoshihide's daughter — art at the cost of everything human

literarySources.title: The Sorrows of Young Werther
literarySources.author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
literarySources.passage: The fountains of the great, deep, joyful Springs of Hope have dried up within me.
literarySources.passageContext: Werther to Wilhelm — the moment of emotional collapse as passion overwhelms purpose
